review_text: Don't recommend to take this class. People who had taken this class told me that it would be a good elective, but it wasn't -- exam questions are tricky and professor doesn't know how to design exams.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Poorly designed quizzes and midterm. Otherwise, the class is worse than mediocre. The problem is that professor doesn't teach you anything during lectures and then asks you questions on midterms that are not even doable in an hour. No clue what the quizzes are for for each assignment. 6 assignments, 6 class works, 6 quizzes, midterm, final - bad!!!

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: This is my first time writing a review on RMP because it's the first class I've taken where the material wasn't actually taught. The midterm and quizzes are poorly written, and with Tarik essentially running the class, it's no surprise it has turned out so lackluster.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: If you do an assignment perfectly you get an 80 + your score on the quiz. Quizzes are way too hard and the midterm was super specific trivia/gotcha questions that barely applied what we learned. Instead of applying the android dev skills we are learning, we are expected to memorize tiny specific details for quizzes and exams. Don't take this class.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Painfully difficult class to succeed in.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Assignments are straightforward at times, but sometimes they require knowledge beyond the scope of what is taught in the class. Midterms and quizzes are tough. My guess is that the TAs, not Professor Nijorn himself, create the midterm and quiz questions. This might explain why many students perform poorly on these assessments.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Exams and quizzes are made by Tarik and other TAs, but Tarik can't create effective midterm questions. Many students did poorly on the midterm, and having a quiz for each assignment feels excessive, especially given the poorly crafted questions. Avoid this class if possible; it's frustrating to pay tuition for a class that isn't properly taught.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Not an easier elective as it used to be. He made the class more difficult, and I don't think he really knows the stuff he's teaching well. Midterm was very difficult, and I can't imagine how difficult the final is going to be.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Lecture heavy--Graded by few things

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Nirjon was good, no complaints. His lectures could be boring but they basically give you a walkthrough for the assignments which build off the last.  Class is easy if you attend lecture and complete the "Class Works".  As stated below grading was a little frustrating as it took all of class period for the TA to get to you. Overall great class
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Graded by few things

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: programming assignments were fair and not very difficult, they are pretty much cumulative and so is the final project. Dry lectures, but nice fella. the grading system for the assignments was pretty frustrating b/c they need to be manually checked off but the prof or TA and you end up sitting around waiting. exam questions try to trip you up though

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: This is an easy class if you put in the work. And you will be learning more on your own than from the professor. But don't be mistaken, you will get a lot out of this.   By the end I had a functional mobile application that I am very proud of.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Nirjon was great!! I genuinely learn SO MUCH in this class, but hardly felt stressed about it. We spent the class learning about API's, databases, sensors, and we ended by learning how to program an Alexa (so cool). It is all tied together in a final project that you work on throughout the year. Such a good talking point for job interviews. 10/10!!

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: His lectures were concise and homework assignments (3) were straightforward. A lot of time during class to work on Class Work assignments and Professor Nirjon always seemed eager to help on any assignments/project components. Midterm and Final Exam were open-book and easy. He's understanding and lenient, I would definitely take this class again!

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Nirjon tries to make sure the students are learning.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Nirjon is alright. 590 was the Android Dev. class and the assignments were time consuming but interesting and worth it. The quizzes however were ridiculous. Nobody did well on them and they quizzed us on strangely specific things. The latter half of the semester was just graduate student presentations (ugh) but overall a relatively easy A/A-
rating_tags: ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria--LECTURE HEAVY

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: goat. super chill, derives ideas bottom up. dense but well organized lectures

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: The exams for this course are difficult if you don't have a very strong math foundation. However, the assignments and teamwork are very helpful. You can learn a lot from them.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lecture heavy--Test heavy

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Do not take this class.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Prof. Oliva is fantastic. Amazingly intelligent, articulate, and he stands out as one of my favorite professors at Carolina. That being said, he teaches a difficult course—be prepared to work. There will not be much hand holding and lectures will focus on key theoretical concepts instead of implementation.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Junier Oliva is by far one the best professors I have ever had. The class itself (Machine Learning) was extremely difficult, requiring somewhere about 5-10 hours per lecture to supplement, understand, and digest the material, especially for some later lectures. However Oliva makes sure to teach the nuance and it makes for a very eye-opening course.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lecture heavy--Graded by few things

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: He covered a good number of introductory ML topics but I don't think I took away much from the course in terms of understanding. Inconsistent grading criteria and a large gap in knowledge expected between class and the final project and exam. Other than bits of theory and derivations, I almost feel like self-study would be more productive.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: The professor is passionate about the subject but he doesn't realize it is under the CS department. The only coding component is Google Colab assignments you'll figure out on your own. Lectures and the midterm are entirely math-based with no programming instruction. Avoid if you want to learn practical Python skills for building and training models

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Definitely not an undergrad level course. You need a good foundation in stats, cal, and linear algebra to take this course. Otherwise, I don't think you would understand much of what he is saying. So I rec you don't take this class if you don't have enough prerequisites. Otherwise, he is nice and passionate about ML.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Amazing lectures --Lecture heavy

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: What's the probability of this professor understanding that the students taking the class have other things to do? Probably another annoyingly long mathematical equation involving various interpretations that would be infinitely easier to understand if the professor actually taught the class instead of dumping random equations every lecture.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: He's nice and enjoys his specialization but he's teaching this course to undergrads at a grad level. Lectures filled with formulas that nobody understands how to work with. Idk how to actually train a model, what a tensor is, or how to use tensorflow. Only take this if you wanna research. Don't take if you wanna be an ML engineer, Udemy is better.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Participation matters--Group projects

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: One of those classes where you go to lecture and watch as 1 person understands everything, and the professor takes this to mean that everybody gets it.   This is a MATH/STOR course, not a COMP course. Heavy on the symbols, low on connections to real life. Little programming.   Strong assumption that you memorized all of LA, Probability, and Calc 3.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Homework and tests could be stressful for this course but the teacher is very approachable and responsive to students' questions and concerns. He seems to be very passionate about his field and is an excellent resource for anyone who really wants to learn

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: This is a GRAD level class not undergrad. He taught the exact same material as his COMP 755. Possibly the worst comp class I have had at UNC. In order to do well on exams/HW you have to already know ML/LinAlg well. He has no problem assigning an exam, project, presentation, and homework all the same week. He curves but not worth the stress at all
rating_tags: Group projects--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Szafir was denied tenure at CU Boulder in March of 2021. Instead of fulfilling his teaching duties for the remainder of the semester he offloaded lectures to TAs, stopped grading and stopped answering emails. He quiet quit before the term even had a name. Dan Szafir is still a laughing stock back here and we'll always remember it despite formality.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: This is a review for COMP 581 - Intro to Robotics Great class with hands-on group projects where you build your lego robot and program it to complete tasks. The material covered in the lectures and exams is relevant to help you build your robot.  My only complaint is that sometimes the lectures move slowly covering basic introductions for too long.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Why are there negative reviews? Because there were no quizes or exams, and no one studied regularly. When professor asked students questions, no one answered because they did not study. Because of this, Professor would never offer a course again where there is no quiz or exams. And its the students fault.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Definitely one of the few professors who really care about students.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Participation matters--Group projects

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: A hidden gem in the CS department!

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: One major project over the course of an entire semester was a bit underwhelming.  We wasted a lot of time doing nothing really besides talking and spinning our wheels. The class was easy, so it gets some points from me on that side, but the course wasn't good.  I had harder classes as a freshmen.  Dan is a nice guy though. All around average.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Awesome professor. Learned a lot about research from this course. Doing the research project was fun.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Human Robot Interaction course. A research based course. Awesome Professor, good as a Kris Jordan. No midterms, quizes or exams. Projects, discussions, papers and presentations. If you are interested in an intro to research course and research related to Robotics, this course is for you.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: This review is actually for COMP 581 which was not listed. Professor Szafir was an excellent intro to robotics professor. The most challenging part about the course was the robotics assignments in which you have to design your own controller for a robot. However, pay attention in class and you will be fine as he gives EC and allows retakes.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: I'm confounded as to why Dan Szafir was the professor for VR and Game Dev. He had no prior experience with game development and his knowledge of VR was only conceptual in nature. All of the technical skills needed to build our assignments were self-taught. Dan also had no examples of his own VR work to show us at all, only previous student work

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: I feel like a barely learned anything from Professor Szafir's class besides just, "Oh look,this engineering concept from a previous course applies here too, but only sort of and to actually implement it, it requires information never covered in class at all" Basically, prepare to teach yourself half of what you'll need on your own. Lazy Professor.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: She's an amazing lecturer. Yes, she might come off as rude but she is genuinely not trying to be based on my long conversations with her. She helped me a lot with understanding NLP concepts. She's definitely a gem of the CS department given her experience and the fact that she takes her job seriously.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Made the course a lot difficult. No lecture recordings, no practice for exams, homeworks are difficult. I would avoid taking a course with her.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Participation matters--Group projects

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: The content of the course is good. The Professor was rude.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: A good lecturer, but not a good person. Half of the class could not get their projects right, and fundamental mistakes were caught. It proves how much less support she gave the class. Dont take.

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: She can clearly explain concepts fairly well. She is incredibly unresponsive, though. She mostly uses Piazza for group discussions, however we don't get an answer for days or sometimes even weeks. There are some challenging concepts in the subject, therefore having a supporting lecturer would be more beneficial.
rating_tags: Group projects--Lecture heavy--Graded by few things

university: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
review_text: Dont take unless you are ready to go through some brutal crazy hard stuff.

By far the worst experience I've had taking a class at Pitt. Projects were the busiest, I often got the highest scores, but truth be told I didn't learn anything about AI from the projects. Easily the worst organized class at Pitt.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Tough grader

review_text: Not harder in terms of content than 1550 or 1501, but get ready to spend time 20+ hour per project in the Python debugger. Lost some blood and sleep worrying about the projects, but at the end of the day, I'm glad I worked through them. Tough grader, but curves assignments and exams somewhat. Good lecturer and kind-hearted imo too.
Worst class ever, absolutely horrible in every aspect.

Don't believe anyone who says that her style of teaching works for this class. She simply wasn't prepared with any of the material and wasn't prepared to teach this class. With that said, she could have taken measures to make it a better experience, but instead the exams, projects and grading were made super-ambiguous and extremely difficult.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Tough grader

review_text: No doubt this class was difficult, but her style of teaching worked for me, but that might not be true for everyone, so its really going to depend on the individual. Her heart is in the right place and she clearly cares about teaching. Assignment 1 is really cool and interesting, but the 2nd project was pretty confusing. Would recommend

Lectures are clueless just like all the students. Projects are literally the hardest of all the CS classes I've ever taken. Exams are the most unpredictable. Everything is graded so harshly in this class. That's literally all you need to know about this class. Take it at your own risk.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Skip class? You won't pass.--Tough grader

review_text: I will give Dr. Walker the benefit of the doubt that it is her first time teaching this course, so I accept the issues with the course content. However, this does not make up for the extreme difficulty of both exams and projects throughout the semester as well as large amounts busy work. Take the course in a year or two once it has been figured out
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Tough grader

review_text: Erin Walker is simply not a good teacher. She can't teach the subject of Artificial Intelligence, or probably anything related to Computer Science. She can't effectively explain course concepts by herself, almost like she's learning about them along with us! I will give her a 2 only because she actually shows some signs of mercy every now and then.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Tough grader

review_text: This AI is literally harder than 1550 and 1501. First class where I felt like all my efforts had to go into AI, otherwise Id easily fail. She posts homework late (yet still expects them on time), grades projects like Satan, and has no qualities of a good Pitt cs professor. DO NOT TAKE AI WITH HER.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Skip class? You won't pass.--Tough grader

review_text: Definitely take it if you want to destroy half your brain cells. More than half the class has no idea what's going on and the assignments are the hardest ever. The hard part is not even AI stuff. Projects are super ambiguous and unorganized. Heard she's ok for HCI though. Extremely tough grader.

Would not recommend. Extremely unorganized, the class is extremely boring to the point where counting ceiling tiles is more entertaining. Assignments are unnecessarily difficult and much of the class is spent doing worksheets that we do not finish. Grading on exams and projects was so ambiguous that I thought it might have depended on the weather

You're gonna have a bad time. I'm not sure what she's like for HCI, but this AI class blows anything I've taken at Pitt out of the water. She at least cares & is a nice person, which prevents me from giving a 1. Her projects (in my opinion) are much more difficult than that of 1501 or 1550 (both with Farnan for reference).

I've had Dr. Walker for 2 different classes. This was her first time teaching AI, and so there were some issues with clarity, organization, etc. so I think this might contribute to difficulty. The class is extremely difficult and she does try her best to help students (maybe next semester it'll be better).Projects were the hardest I've had.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: dont take her, hardest teacher ive ever had at pitt and i still dont know anything about AI!!! really bad grading criteria

Blame Pitt for giving Mrs. Walker this class to teach. If you look at her other school ratings, she tought HCI. But since Pitt is so desperate for teachers, they gave her AI. She is clearly out of her depth. She isn't stupid but being able to teach a subject requires a deep familiarity w/ it. In the future, only take a class of hers if it is in HCI
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Tough grader

review_text: take if you like TONS of busy work. expect 3x the work of any other class and very harsh grading. she skipped at least 4 classes for 'PhD commitments'. as the substitute, the TA sat in corner, silent for full 75 minutes. otherwise, TA was invisible, except when nitpicking & slashing points from projects. you wont get an A, take if you hate your GPA

Doesn't teach. 85% of lecture time is spent on completing worksheets. Claims you learn more, but most fail. The grading: very ambiguous & unforgiving. Class becomes a gauntlet of busy work that too concerned with finishing, results in very little learning. If you don't attend, finish worksheet & question each class, you lose points. Avoid.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Tough grader

review_text: Assignment/exam grading was way, way too subjective and unclear. I found that the more work I put into the class as the semester went on, the poorer my grades became in this class. Don't expect any grades to be returned to you in a timely manner. By finals week, I only had roughly 25% of my grades done. Avoid this professor at all costs.

This was Prof. Walker's first semester, so there was more administrative overhead than I expected. Other than that this was one of my favorite courses @ Pitt! Walker is super passionate and deeply knowledgable about HCI, and that translated to her lectures. She also focused on learning by doing, not just talking, which was effective for me.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Participation matters

review_text: good luck to anyone taking him. you get absolutely no guidance on labs/assignments, lectures are very monotonous/boring and have LITTLE TO NO correlation to what is on the exams and assignments. you are basically teaching yourself in this class
The course material is pretty dry but he is just not a good lecturer. He is not very difficult but you def won't learn much from his classes.

I am taking the class CS2756. The professor is patient and approachable. The lecture overall is interesting. Some concepts have been covered in other lectures but he provides different views. The homework questions are very helpful for understanding. The professor gave good advice on course project.

He explains things clearly in the lectures, and is very patient in anwering questions during office hours. I learned a lot from this class. The class covers many topics but is relatively easy. The design of labs and lectures seem to be a bit disconnected though.

Jia does not seem to care about this class. We were told to use Ed Discussions for posting questions, but I have not seen him answer any threads all semester. The recitation and project requirements were very vague and the TAs were no help. The class itself you can pass without going to class, and after one lecture you won't want to go anyways.
His class is a joke. Easy but such a hassle. Unclear requirements, never responds to emails or a discussion thread he told us to use, etc. Just shows up and does the bare minimum.

The lectures can be dry and drag on, but overall the class was bearable and the slides/recitations were helpful enough to complete the assignments. The TA was a bit disengaged throughout the semester. Exams were online and weighted minimally, so most of the grade is based on assignments and the term project.
The entire class has felt unorganized and it's difficult to get ahold of either the professor or TA for clarification. Really do not recommend him

Lectures are pretty dry. He's not super accessible and seems to not even be knowledgable as to what happens with labs and assignments. All assignments seem hastily put together with often unclear instructions or details on what's expected in the submission. I get the impression he doesn't care about teaching at all and just wants to do his research

Xiaowei is an OK prof. You can tell that he's super smart, but he goes through dense lecture material really quickly so it's easy to get lost or fall behind. However, his assignments don't go as in-depth as his lectures do, so it's not hard to do well. He is also always willing to answer questions and is very caring and accommodating as a person.

Dr. Kovashka knows a lot about the Deep Learning field, and may not always be able to simplify the content to a beginner level super well. However, as a CS student, I believe you are doing yourself a disservice by not taking this class. The content is super relevant and Kovashka will answer any questions you may have and is very accomodating.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Graded by few things

review_text: Hands-down an incredible professor. MATLAB homeworks are very time-consuming and math-heavy, and the pencil-and-paper exams are extremely difficult, but both Kovashka and the TA (Erhan Unal) graded very generously. I HIGHLY recommend taking linear algebra beforehand; otherwise, be prepared to do a lot of catch-up when it comes to the math.

The course is extremely basic for a graduate course. People without any background on Image Processing can take the course. If you are a graduate student(MS or PhD), you might be disappointed. The home work is more of a deep learning tutorial rather than a proper vision course. The math is minimal and there is no building up models from the start.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: It's true, the slides have a lot of math, but if you've taken linear algebra, calculus 3, and an optimization class, it's pretty simple math. A ton of homework, and mostly theory based where you implement the actual algorithms. If you're competent, it's an easy A. If you're incapable of doing math, it's a B.

Get ready for a lot of math, including calculus and linear alg. I felt she went into too much depth with the math. But the tests were surprisingly easy and not so intense. You won't use libraries--you'll write ML algorithms in MATLAB from scratch. Very theoretical/research-oriented, does not teach how ML is done in industry. Very fun, caring person

Adriana's computer vision is great! Homeworks takes some time(4-6 hours for the hardest one), but they are doable. Tests are easy, no tricks, you can get around 90 as long as you truly understand the materials. Review the ppt, go to office hours if there is confusions, and you are good to go. I really learned a lot from this course. Recommended!

Computer Vision!  This class is very difficult, but I learned a lot . As someone else said, there is a TON of math-heavy programming homework, and exams are very hard. But... it was all graded very leniently. Show up, put in a serious effort, and you will be okay.
LOTS of homework
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: 435 w/ Sanjay is one of the best structured classes I've ever taken. It does take a good bit of effort, but the lectures/assignments are really interesting and even fun. I did think some of the coding questions on the final were harder than they should have been, but overall I really enjoyed this class-- recommend it for a tech elective!

Class-wise: clear/detailed slides, practice exams exactly like the real ones, and github actions tests on the PAs make them pretty easy (1-2 hours each + OH). I definitely learned a lot! As a prof: def one of the most chill/funny in the entire CS dept. I think he's chronically online or smth LOL just like us fr. also very kind and helpful in OH!
Everybody knows how good Sanjay is. Take him if you can!!!

Grading works differently in this class than in the normal grading system, but his rubrics are always very clear and specific. Overall, a very easy class if you follow the directions exactly. He has lots of industry experience and gives lots of helpful advice about getting into the games industry. The textbook is optional.

Sanjay is such a great guy. Lectures are laid back and informative. Office hours are even more casual. Students feel free talking to Sanjay about anything. Material is interesting for the most part. PAs and exams are all manageable.

Great professor! Very informational lectures. The assignments are not easy; you're expected spend 5-10 hours each week on it, but they are well designed to help you learn. Would strongly recommend those interested in gameplay programming to take it.
Nice person but a really bad teacher. Manymany homework, and hard exams. Would NOT recommend

Unreal class was amazing for its first semester. Labs were split with 40 min follow-along video, and 4-8 hours of work each week. A lot of first-hand experience with coding in unreal. Q's were answered quickly on Piazza, and OH was easily available. Everyone is provided a VM to run UE5, so accessibility was great. Take this class if you can!

Sanjay is a good prof and really kind, but to be honest I really don't recommand this compiler course. It sounds cool when you first heard about it, but things really fresh your mind when you start taking this course and find how abstract and unuseful the knowledge is. Seriously, I started realizing how these things are used after the midterm.

Sanjay is the best. Pro C++ has been one of my favorite classes at USC. Homeworks really drilled the concepts and the midterm and final was super practical. The class was not easy, but Sanjay taught the material well, engaged the class so it was extremely doable. The PAs were a bit tough, but just doing them was strong prep for the exams.

Had a really good experience in ITP 380 w/ Sanjay, so I decided to take ITP435: Pro C++, and this class is even better. Not only does course content cover things you would not normally learn w/ classes like CS103/CS104/ITP365, its also is so applicable and intuitive. No other class at USC has prepped me better for Industry than ITP435.

Easily the best Professor I've had at USC! ITP380 is a really interesting class and he does an excellent job teaching it. The assignments are straight forward and easy to follow for the most part, and are also extremely cool. Take this class if you're willing to put in 3-4 hours of work every week and have some C++ experience.

Easily the best professor I've had at USC. He's a real pro in the gaming industry, and knows exactly what he's talking about. Even though 380 is assignment heavy, the assignments are 1) Easily doable if you follow the PDF, 2) There's a unique regrade policy, and 3) Him and his TAs are easily accessible.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: He's hilarious and did a great job during COVID-19 online classes. Sanjay is brilliant so the content's easy for him. But, he'll happily answer any questions if he glosses difficult over something in class.

Sanjay is an awesome teacher for compilers and c++. He's always down to crack jokes and even streams on twitch. Lots of homework, but it also counts for a large portion of your grade. If you like to skip class, the powerpoint slides are excellent and have step-by-step instructions for all topics covered.

Sanjay is an awesome professor! Highly recommend ITP380 if you want to make cool projects in C++ and learn more about the video game industry. You make one new game per week, and you have a week to work more on the assignments and ask for a regrade.

Sanjay is a really great lecturer. He's super animated and has the rare ability of working humor into the otherwise dry sections of the IT/CS material he's tasked with teaching. His office hours get super crowded, so plan ahead if you need help, if you show up at the last minute you'll run out of time.

Sanjay is amazing. The labs (the best part of this class) are interesting and require you to put some serious efforts into them. Getting A is not hard because of the new grading method. This is the class to take if you are interested in gameplay programming.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: Course materials weren't too hard(pretty easy actually), but it took me a good amount of time to finish the weekly lab assignments. Overall a good introductory-level class. He gave solid opinions/feedback and was willing to spend time to help his students out. Attendance was only mandatory for the lab session.

Awesome professor! Helpful at office hours, always cracks jokes during lecture, and is super chill. He's very fair and the ITP 435 is pretty manageable if you start PAs early. Most importantly, he helps you understand what working industry is like and will answer you honestly with no BS. Definitely take any classes you can with him.

He is a respectful and caring professor, willing to debug code for students on piazza. However, he assigns way too much homework. There are TWELVE PAs for the class, most of them took me 8 - 10 hours to complete. Thus, this class easily becomes the most demanding class. I also find it hard to understand some of the concepts he presented in lecture

My favorite professor at USC. His class is useful and well-organized. One of the few classes at USC that would prepare you for tech interviews. Easy A if you do all the assignments and review them before exams.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: One of the best professors at USC. Clear lectures, reasonable assignments, fair exams, and a humorous teaching style. The course covers many useful aspects of modern C++. Some of the materials are harder, but by doing the assignments you can soon get hold of it. If you pay attention to the lecture well and ace all the assignments, an A is secured.

Sanjay is a great lecturer. Programming assignments could take many hours so plan your time wisely. Tests were a little tough but he curved the final grade. I learned a lot of new C++ concepts that were not covered in CS103/104. Definitely recommended! Also don't cheat on homework. He uses Moss to catch plagiarism.
rating_tags: LOTS OF HOMEWORK--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: Sanjay is by far one of the best professors Ive ever had. He was one of the first lecturers who made game programming click, which convinced me to continue pursuing Computer Science (Games). Im very thankful I had his classes. Even as a graduate, when I need to review any concepts, I usually re-read his books.

Very useful class if you want to ace those game programming interviews. Not the most engaging lecturer, but super chill. Goes through material super slowly. First midterm was easy, second midterm was low-key kinda hard, final was in-between. Otherwise, not a class you will stress too much about.
If you've never taken Sanjay's class, don't tell people you study games at USC
Not the most charismatic lecturer, but he does his best to get everyone engaged. He's always accessible if anyone has questions, and has a very well organized class.

The midterm is a little bit tricky but if you listen carefully in the first half of the semester it won't be a problem. Homework only contains 5 labs and they are so easy if you follow instructions in the lab guide. You could use UE4 engine to write your own game as project. Better if you know about linear algebra and C++ programing skills.
Great teacher
He is a funny guy who likes video games and to help his students. Honestly one of the nicest people, let alone professors, that I have ever met.
Favorite class & teacher so far
Good teacher
Cool, funny guy. Makes class fun and interesting. You do the work, you'll get an A. Exactly what a professor should be.

The nature of Pro C++ is to expose you to different techniques in the C++ language beyond your fundamental data structures. The programming assignments are really well-designed, and Sanjay is just a cool guy. Lectures are long and might put you to sleep, but the slides are really good that it doesn't matter anyway. Exams aren't hard.
Great teacher, explains everything clearly. The class was a very good learning experience, very worthwile.
Decent professor decent class, better than most progeamming classes.
The worst professor ever in USC. Do not take him

Great professor, clear, easy to understand. Helpful if you ran into a problem. Good teacher overall. But, I feel like the lectures and lab did not contribute as much as I expected. Having previous understanding of the math involved from the CG class, and basic geometry from HS, I didn't learn much new. Labs were following a easy tutorial.

Sanjay's lab assignments are so well designed. The learning curve starts easy but ramps up as class goes on. Paying attention in lecture and studying outside of class is definitely needed for quizzes and midterm.

If you're a games major or interested in games, take Sanjay's ITP380 class ASAP. He teaches you material you need to know for interviews esp in linear algebra for games and programming. Can't give Sanjay higher than a 5 so time to rate him hot.

Sanjay is probably the best professor I've had at USC.  He really hit the nail on saying how USC math makes you hate math lol.  In fact I found out later on, all of the linear algebra I needed for industry was learned through this class.  Take this class very seriously because half the test questions are from interviews.
Awesome teacher, definitely deserving of his 5.0 rating.  He should teach other teacher's how to teach.

Sanjay is THE game programing professor at USC. His lectures are informative and he occasionally tells an anecdote from industry. His labs are well designed to build up the skills a game programmer needs.

Sanjay is a great professor who really helps you learn. His classes aren't tough to do well in but I learned more from him than any other professor at USC. He's really good at presenting material in a way that makes you interested in it and he gives you tons of resources to do well. He's also extremely accessible and helpful.
He's silly, comedic, and useful.

Sanjay is a great teacher and I think he is changing USC's games program for the better. It took me a while to figure him out, but now that I have, I have a ton of respect for him. Talk to him, listen to him, and realize that he is a nerd just like you and me - but a very knowledgeable. You won't find a better professor in the program.

Sanjay is an awesome professor .. His lectures can get boring, but still, pay attention because he gives you info you'll need for the test. The projects are fun and he is very insightful whenever you need help.

Sanjay provides insight into the wonderful world of game programming. His lectures are almost poetic in nature.  Sanjay's delivery is most appealing.  Unfortunately his teaching skill does not translate to SC2. He sucks at that.

The class feels very disorganized. The class material is poorly documented and kinda confusing. The homework contains multiple ambiguities and errors. The TA could not even come up with the correct code himself yet graded us harshly. Also, I do not find the class material interesting.
It's a lot of learning but it comes with a lot of work
This is the WORST class I have ever taken in my life. The university made me have a mandatory counselor meeting because of how stressed this class made me. It's not worth it, pick ANYONE else.

Hardest class I've ever taken by far. Taught like a math class, but with programming HW. If you are comfortable with vector calculus, linear algebra, and probability, you can get a ton out of this class; If not I would definitely look elsewhere. I second what others have said about the HW & exams.  HUGE final curve. Enjoyed the class overall.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: There are better ML professors who teach more with less pain. His lectures are hard to follow with arbitrary math terms. Homework takes 40+ hours for a B, exams average 15-30%, and it is a ton of stress

Antithesis of an easy A. Slow grading = little feedback before exams, no cheat sheets on very difficult tests, and homework code could take hours to run. He is very accessible and passionate, so you could learn a lot. Linear algebra, probability, multivariable calculus familiarity needed. Would only recommend if machine learning is a priority.
rating_tags: Graded by few things--Accessible outside class--Online Savvy

review_text: I like Dr. Ruozzi, he's a really cool guy but man he does not go easy on you. He's a very smart guy who knows what he's talking about, but I don't think he knows how to bring things down to an intro level class. Homeworks are hard and time-consuming, and the exams are awful with a massive curve. Love him, but there are easier professors.

Stats for AI and ML had super challenging homeworks and the lectures were difficult to understand because it is content that is not well documented in textbooks and youtube videos, he assumes that you already have mastery of algos, prob and stats, and multivariable calculus at start of the course, a lot of people dont have this
rating_tags: Tough grader--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: Prof. Ruozzi is an amazing professor overall; however, there's a big caveat when it comes to how he teaches 4375, which is that he teaches it like you're grad students. It's therefore extraordinarily difficult, being easily the hardest class I've ever taken.  You'll learn a lot, though! Plus, he curves really hard, so trying your best helps :)
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Amazing lectures --Lots of homework

review_text: GOAT of a Professor. Really smart, nice and knowlegable. Always answer questions. Tough course but definitely worth it.

His lectures go in-depth and are very good. Don't save the projects until the last 2 hours! They are much more enjoyable if you spend more time on them, lots to learn and feel satisfied about. Tests are very hard, for good reason. The average midterm/final scores were about 25%. Make friends in the class and study together! Difficult, but rewarding
rating_tags: Tough grader--Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback

review_text: Very tough class, need to spend 4 to 5 days to complete each assignment. It's worth it.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: His lectures are awesome and descriptive(math heavy). Second part of the class(after midterm) becomes more fast paced. He makes sure that you study well for exam by giving conceptual homeworks. Some of his favourite topics- Convex Optimization, Duality, VC dimension, Gaussian mixture. My advice- Use office hours, piazza & class hours to ask doubts.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Amazing lectures --Test heavy

review_text: Very tough!
Pick him if you want to learn avoid if you just need a grade

Ruozzi teaches ML as optimization and does not shy away from the theory, so this course is math-heavy. It is the hardest course I've taken at UTD but also one of the most fulfilling. The homeworks usually took me ~20-35 hours each. The workload is gruesome if you want to do well, but if you survive it you'll be highly knowledgeable in the subject.
If you want to actually learn ML go for his class. Otherwise content on youtube is good enough for you, compared to other profs like Anjum and Anurag.
He's by far the best professor for Machine Learning you can find at UTD. Seems like vibhav is overrated and Ruozzi is underrated. Makes you work for it but this course will be worth it.

Professor is knowledgeable on the subject. The class is difficult. You need to know MatLab/Python as you'll be implementing the algorithms you see in class yourself for the homeworks. Don't expect to score high on the exams (iirc he said the average for the midterm was in the 30s-40s). Curves. If you just want a peek at ML, might pick someone else.

This class is definitely difficult, and the really motivated will find plenty of room to go very deep into the material. The professor is highly invested in the subject. Just know what you are getting into, and give yourself time to learn the material.

He is a phenomenal professor for ML. He makes sure that you understand everything and answers all your questions patiently. The assignments are really time consuming and tough but, they are completely worth doing because, the amount of knowledge you gain from them is very high. His exams are really tough!

The professor is very clear, knows the subject and makes it engaging. He is always willing to take questions and is accessible. The course is math intensive but doable if you are willing to work hard. One of the best courses I've taken!
rating_tags: Get ready to read--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Amazing lectures

review_text: Very knowledgeable and answers plenty of questions. However it was by far the hardest class I had ever taken. Exams are very difficult and grading is tough.
The toughest class I've had. No exam but the assignments are heavy. Consider carefully before taking this class
rating_tags: Get ready to read--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--LECTURE HEAVY

review_text: Took Statistical Methods in Data Science, and believe me this is the toughest topic that I've ever came across. Maybe it suits best for PhD folks, but not for Masters! No exams but those 7 assignments are no less than 7 final exams! Beware, if you like the subject, ignore me, else please save yourself.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--SO MANY PAPERS

review_text: course content is tough and math heavy, requiring solid math foundation in algebra, analysis, probability, convex optimization and writing proofs. homework load is very heavy and tough, too. So is exam. Good part is that professor is helpful and give timely feedback in response to questions.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Tests are tough

review_text: He is a really good professor, but he grades really tough and gives a lot of problem sets and the exams are very difficult.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--Get ready to read--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: very knowledgeable and patient in answering questions of all kind. course content is very hard and math heavy. was unable to understand everything and struggled throughout the whole semester. fortunately managed to understand a couple of the most important concepts, which was enough to complete all the assignments.

review_text: One of the most amazing professor of Machine Learning at UT Dallas. Learnt a lot doing assignments.  Would definitely recommend to a person who wants to go deep into machine learning.
One of the most knowledgeable professors in the field of Machine Learning. Very quick to respond. Assignments take up your time and exams are tough as well.
VERY TOUGH

Explains the theory behind every algo with examples. HWs will make you really work hard. Always available to clarify doubts. Exams will be very tough. But good thing is grading is relative and if you took the lectures and HWs seriously you need not even study for exams. Best professor to understand the subject and not just learn to apply diff algo.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: I took Elem. D.S. and Algorithms (CS 2401) with her. She is a caring professor, but she lectures with powerpoint and is quiet. There were short pop quizzes, but they weren't stressful. If you read the OpenDSA textbook and understand it all (even before the class), this course and lab will be easy for you.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: You can't miss her class, she has iclicker and does regular quizzes during class. Be it the last 10 minutes too. She has 3 exams and she posts previous exam questions/answers but they aren't similar to what's on the exams. The exams have about 2 questions that count for about 50% of the total exam grade, they're coding segments.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Participation matters--Beware of pop quizzes

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: (CS2401)Akbar fails to communicate effectively what she is talking about ,is very unmotivating, hard to hear and often only covers what is in her slides. Additionally she spends too long on the easier-to-grasp topics but completely brushes over the harder material. The final exam passing grade had to be brought down from a 70%  to a 54 by her.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: (CS2401) Couldn't hear the lectures very much, kinda all over the place IMO but the actual course was nicely structured and she provided many resources. Random pop quizzes or class activities here and there for attendance. Verryyyy generous grading, definitely prioritizes understanding, and is super accessible out of class.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Ms. Akbar is an excellent teacher who explains concepts clearly and provides free textbooks and resources for further practice outside of class. She's very organized with Blackboard, uploading lecture materials, slides, and important task dates. Uses surprise quizzes to reinforce learning, and attendance is mandatory for success in her class.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Her lectures were a bit boring but good. She gave out optional homework so we could practice. The exams were the perfect difficulty. The labs were really good and my favorite part of the class. She is very understanding.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: I took CS 2401 but wasnt an option to put it. She gives off nice mom vibes. kind of hard to hear. Lectures are kind of dragged out and repetitive which ig was good to listen then zone out a bit. I mainly learned through labs which is what I mainly do anyways. First portion of the semester is a review of CS1. Love her good vibes tho. would recommend

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Too many surprises. Jumps right in without a refresher of CS1 concepts; be prepared to teach yourself. Couldn't hear or understand the professor when she was lecturing. No explanations, just textbook problems. Practice tests contained almost nothing that the final test would. Will not inform you if you are falling behind. Avoid.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Took CS 2401 with Dr. Akbar. Alex Vargas was a great TA, always available and glad to help. Sometimes they do get behind on grading, so it is important to give them reminders. There are pop quizzes in class that cannot be made up for so make sure to go to class. Best way to study for exams is to review sample midterm questions that she posts.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Professional, inspirational, and kind are just a few qualities that Dr. Akbar display. She is an excellent lecturer and also helps in her office hours with a positive attitude. Additionally her eye bright can enthral to anyone. Highly recommended if you want to learn from the best. She is an excellent professor and person

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Dr. Akbar is great! She explains everything very clearly and if you didn't understand something its very easy to reach her outside of class. It is a little tough to see how you're doing in her class, but as long as you turn everything in you should be good.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Caring--Accessible outside class

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Monika is a great professor, she explained all of our covered topics very simply and easy to understand, and with a little bit of effort within each class, all of the quizzes and midterms were a breeze. So basically, as long as you try a least a little you're bound to understand the material deeply.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: One of my favorite teachers throughout taking CS. Goes in depth in lectures and gives very good examples. Her voice is kind of quiet and monotone, but I never found myself dosing off in her classes or anything. Learned java basics very quick in her class. Really cares about her students, remembered my name by the end of the first week. Love her!
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures--Caring

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: She is really caring and charasmatic. She is also very understanding and helpful there is a handful of asignements but nothing to crazy.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: She was a good teacher. I personally would get lost but if I sent her an email for help she would take a few days to reply but would help. The test aka midterms would be a bit hard but she did have reviews and review sessions
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Professor is average because you can't use the lectures she gives in the lab. In exams, she provides a sample exam but does require the textbook and to pay attention in lectures. Attendance is a big part of the grade and the lecture and lab. She's there to help if you seek her out.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: This is a great professor. This is a great chance for you to learn about the very basics of JAVA and computer science.

review_text: She gives good lectures, however they do not really translate into the lab. In the lecture, we mainly focused on algorithms or plans for the code.
