review_text: Akbar's lectures were clear and easy to understand and she often drew diagrams to further explain the topics discussed. Test are insanely easy and also have extra credit questions for full letter grades worth of extra credit. The lab linked to this course however was insanely hard and forces you to kinda learn on your own for the most part.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: She explains things very well and she cares about her students. She has an accent that sounds funny sometimes.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: her lectures were hard to follow expect allot of lab assignments in which will take up most of your time.  I would focus allot on the lab because that is how you learn the programming so you can be ready for the test.  Vocabulary terms expect them on the test.  She does send out the slides on BB so don't take too much notes.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: THE GOAT. Doctor Hossain really makes you understand the material with just his lectures. Not a Lecture person? His website is such an amazing resource for you to study. In my opinion, only those who don't care at all fail

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Great guy, the class was interesting and he taught it well. Solid lecture, no book but he provides videos and readings through his website.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Very nice professor who knows a lot about accounting. If you keep up, you'll understand the material. We had one online test and two on paper, all open book. He gives group problems in class that we turn in after. Grades are based on attendance, exams, and in-class work.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Dr. Hossain made me care about learning AI. His lectures are easy to follow. His exams are easy. Homework are not to difficult. The grad version only consists of reading a paper in pairs and making a 3 minute video about it.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: He is straight forward and clearly states what you need to do and when. An online textbook is used bu he does not rely on it. Lectures are all you need to do good on the class. He is not the kind of professor that makes you suffer on purpose.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Ditto everything everyone else said. He tries to work with the class and often has these moments of hilarious inspiration that come out of nowhere. This guy isn't looking to stand in your way, he just wants to teach you a couple things. I enjoyed his class a lot more than I thought I would. Wish I was able to take his course in person.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: One of the most patient professors I have ever had. Depending on your skill with java the labs will either be easy or they'll be pretty difficult. You don't have to go to lectures since he places his slides on blackboard but he will do exercises in class. Exams can be challenging but he lets you use a cheat sheet. great class.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Dr. Hossain is a great professor.. Exams are difficult, but he lets you use a cheat sheet on every exam. He can and will throw curve balls in the exam, but if you study well that wont be a problem. The labs are challenging, but that's his way of making you a better programmer. Lectures are a must, they're filled to the brim with vital information.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: I would take a class with this professor again, he is very patient with his students and goes over the important parts of the course in detail. Keep up with your work and you'll be happy with the end result.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: This professor has a kind, and old soul. Urban legend has it that he was raised by unicorns, and fairies in a monk monetary. He has enough patience to wait for an eternity. Genius is patience. He is very knowledgeable about the course.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Excellent professor

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Dr. Hossain is a great professor, he is willing to help anyone. His lectures aren't boring. He is really smart and has a funny side. If you know basic programming it is an easy A.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: Awesome professor! He explains very clear and straightforward; no group projects.

university: University of Texas at El Paso
review_text: One of the better professors I've had. He has a good amount of enthusiasm and is fairly easy to follow. His class can be challenging for some but I think he is pretty fair when it comes to grading.

She's a really great professor, but I wish I'd taken a different class with her. We got behind from the first day of lecture, due to her stopping to answer questions for 20+ mins instead of telling people to ask her outside of class. Lectures are not recorded. Be prepared for the tests to be hard. The content itself isn't, but the tests are brutal.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: The class does not have hard subject matter. Most annoying part is the required participation during lecture, but professor Elhabian drops an entire midterm and allows note sheets on exams. It is not a difficult class but the style of lecture may require you to learn the material on your own. This shouldn't be unfamiliar to college students though.

Maybe the worst prof I have ever had. The content isn't too difficult, but her explanations will leave you with 100 more questions. The tests are the bad part of this class. There is no curve, and every question feels like a trick. Toward the end, I stopped going to class and self-studied because I disliked her so much.

Professor Elhabian is incredibly accommodating, sweet, and caring. She drops a midterm, homework, attendance, and gives a generous amount of extra credit. Do not worry about the other reviews, she makes the class easy to pass given you practice and stay proactive.

Very caring professor who wanted us to succeed and provided many extra credit opportunities. Tough exams and attendance was graded. If attendance wasn't graded and exams were not as tough, I would rate 5/5. Definitely a good person. TAs in this course were also great and genuinely cared about students.

She tries to explain things in a way that can be understood, using imagery in her slides. Her slides are a great study resource, and she posts plenty of extra study material. She is super open to feedback on how to help students, and I could tell she wanted her students to succeed. Participation is counted to help students engage in the course.
Very lecture heavy and slow at teaching the material. Overall a easy class but every lecture was a drag and mandatory to attend.

Gives homework in Jupyter Notebooks and there are group projects. The slides just like her 2100 class have all the material on the slides so you can review it. There are companion notebooks for lectures meaning all the notes are pre-done for you. Overall S-tier professor.
Heavy slides with all the information you need. The homework is clear and there are many redundant fallbacks to save your understanding of material. She is forgiving and understanding.

genuinely great professor. changed the class based on feedback, exams were pretty easy. The class covers a pretty large amount of things, and usually the homework was difficult but you don't get a bunch of homework so its pretty manageable. If you're considering taking 4640 with her, do yourself a favor and ignore the 2100 ratings.

Really interesting class but relatively new, Prof Shireen is very knowledgeable in this subject and cares about her students and always adjusts to the feedback given by the students, she has great accommodations for students. Tests where not as bad, study her practice tests they helped a ton in exams. Make sure you are familiar with Jupyter and py.

Don't get me wrong, her teaching style is boring and terrible. But because there were online videos for the content, I could skip the boring lectures, saving me oodles of time. At least if you are good at math this class will be a stress free and easy A, otherwise it may be hell. (she dropped a midterm? so nice, way more accommodating than others)

The professor gives you every chance to succeed in this class, multiple scores are dropped, a midterm score is dropped, an alternate grading scheme is offered at the end of the semester etc... The lecture material for the course is available online. Tests can be tough but the practice tests are similar to the exams, there are no surprises on exams.

She's super nice and accommodating, but unfortunately the class itself is just very boring. I feel like this would be true regardless of the professor, so I feel a little bad giving a 3/5. But anyways, the homework wasn't too bad and the exams were fine as long as you put in the time studying. In 2100, practice makes perfect.

The midterms are worth 30% of your grade and the final is worth 40% or vice versa depending on what gives you a better grade. 70% for tests though is crazy. If you're not a good test taker like me you will suffer. Her tests are like booklets when she hands them out. Her lectures aren't good either and you're better off to watch the videos she has.

Professor Elhabian is kind and caring and is very responsive to feedback. She tries her best to make sure that everybody understands before moving on during lectures and her online videos are concise and great. If you watch her videos and do the practice problems you will most likely get an A.

Shireen clearly cares about student feedback, and has made several changes to the course based on suggestions at the beginning of the semester. I stopped going to class after the first few weeks, because her lectures just weren't worth it. I got by just fine with the videos on Canvas. Homeworks can be rough though.

While the flipped classroom is no longer a thing, you're better off skipping class and getting the information through her online module. Its unfortunate that this class was mandatory as most of the topics she (barely) teaches are better covered in other courses likely required for the CS majors anyways. She does drop a few things and grading is ok

She means well and i really appreciate the fact that she listens to student feedback (no more flipped classroom). I have emailed her before and she is nice and seems to care about her students, but when it comes down to it her style of teaching just really doesnt work well for me.
I hate this class, and don't know how Elhabian got hired. Padding bad reviews.
Yeah just trying to pad the bad reviews and give a recent one because even without the flipped classroom this class is still really bad.
I've emailed her before and she is actually a nice and accommodation person. Her class used to be "flipped" but everyone hated it so she ended up changing it to normal.

Lecture's a waste of time, Students have to correct her on the curriculum she's teaching A LOT. Homework that's weirdly focused on quantity rather than quality but well placed due dates .Curriculum isn't bad, Workload is. She is new and adapting to feedback, but its so hard to see how she was hired.

It is hard to rate this professor in a negative way as she did try to make the class easier but CS2100 was the worst class I have ever had. It is a flipped classroom for all CS2100 classes so you will do most of the learning by yourself. I recommend going to as much TA hours as possible.

I see from other ratings that the flipped classroom is controversial; I personally didn't like it. I think it's far more conducive to subject matter that has a discussion element. CS 2100 is a math class. There's nothing to discuss. Prof. Elhabian tries her best, but she felt a bit out of her depth, and she can be a bit overbearing. She's nice tho.

Despite what a lot of other students are saying, I actually liked the flipped model of the classroom. The pre-lecture videos, in my opinion, were concise and of high quality. I hardly ever attended the in-person lectures, but I guess they're a good resource if you're confused about the subject matter. Tests were hard but the questions were fair.

Teaches in a "flipped" classroom where you teach yourself from her monetized youtube channel and take a mandatory pre-lecture quiz with questions she didnt proof read. Actual lectures taught nothing new yet were mandatory. By far the worst teacher I've had at the U.

Although I strongly disliked this class at the beginning of the semester, professor Elhabian has improved greatly throughout the entire semester, listening and incorporating feedback into her class. I've learned a lot more in the second half of the semester and the class has definitely become more flexible and accessible.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: She is a good person and tried to be accommodating; however, she really just didn't understand what was being taught and would be corrected by the teaching assistants and students from time to time on the material. I honestly blame the College of Engineering for putting her in this bad teaching situation with flipcl. Still 1 due to teaching poorly.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: Professor Elhabian had a rough start, but it was obvious she cared about the students. She had a mid semester feedback survey, and a lot of things in it did get added to the class. For being given such a terrible class format by the SoC, she made it work. her teaching style isn't very engaging, but you can supplement that with lots of help hours.

Although I was not happy with professor Elhabian at the beginning of the semester, but I must say this--she is very caring, understanding and nice Professor who deeply cares about her students and their performance in her class. She takes feedbacks seriously and listens to it.

While the flipped classroom took a little getting used to, the videos she makes for lectures are super helpful and very informative. The way she teaches really helps my understanding of the topics, and the examples she does in class enhance that understanding so much. The grading of the course is very forgiving, especially for what we are learning.
A haiku about CS 2100:  CS Twenty-one  Hundred lectures are all bad  The quizzes suck too

Professor Elhabian is not a good professor and it seems like the whole class is set up for the students to fail. It is a flipped classroom so you're required to teach yourself everything and she constantly gets the material wrong when she finally does go over it in class. She is probably a nice person but unfortunately not very good at her job.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Test heavy

review_text: The class is a "flipped classroom" with very difficult subjects. I am unsure what purpose a professor brings if you have to learn everything outside of class and are expected to master the material on quizzes before having the opportunity to ask questions in class. When you are in class, she makes multiple mistakes and causes confusion for subjects
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: I think Dr. Elhabian is a really good person and is very kind to all her students. However, this class is not designed very well. You have to watch about 4 hours of videos weekly and the quizzes can be difficult. The lectures are simply practice problems, but often have mistakes and lead to further confusion.

As many others said you're teaching yourself. I have no idea why this class is required and only taught by ONE person. Not to mention it's so early that if you commute via transit you may have to wake up really early. Honestly the lectures don't help, I'd just watch other videos.

Never take this class I have learned one thing about this class, and it wasn't from any instruction from the Professor. Her lecture videos are way too long along with the fact that she can't get through a lecture without making multiple mistakes, making it pointless to attend mandatory lectures.

I don't know why I am paying huge tuition if I have to learn everything on my own. I'd really appreciate if the professor would really teach something during the actual lecture rather than leaving everything with the pre lect stuffs!!
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lecture heavy

review_text: Dr. Elhabian's flipped class is a struggle for students. Lengthy videos, unengaging in-person class, and no real instruction lead to stress and frustration. Avoid.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: As a person Dr.Elhabian seems great, but her class structure has been very hard for me to handle. Watching 2+ hours of lecture videos, along side a quiz and book reading two days a week just to be behind in lecture is nothing short of exhausting. The class is also a "flipped" structure meaning that not much is taught in class anyways.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: I've never rated a professor before but this professor is my exception. By far the worst teaching if you could even call it that. The entire class is based on you teaching yourself the material and the mandatory lectures being a review of what you taught yourself. Why this teacher is the only option for a required course is beyond me.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lecture heavy

review_text: I am currently in her class and I hate it with a passion. She assigns 2 hours of videos for homework, along with a quiz that takes 30 minutes, twice a week. Then in class she is an awful lecturer, takes way too long on problems and can't control the room and I end up learning nothing. Don't cover things in class that are on the homework.Overall bad

Shireen is by far the worst lecturer I have ever had in my entire schooling career. You're better off teaching yourself from the textbook than watching any of her lecture videos. In class she incompletely explains practice problems and often writes a bunch of nonsense for easy problems while glossing over ones that require a deeper explanation.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Lecture heavy

review_text: The professor only does practice problems during lecture instead of teaching. She assigns textbook chapters that take about an hour to read in total, as well as 3hrs of video lectures that get mixed into pre-quizzes(2 a week) that so far have had wrong answers for some of the questions. In class she makes mistakes during the practice problems too.
Be prepared to take quizzes where the professor doesn't not teach the material. Quizzes are way too hard and you can spend hours trying to figure out an assignment.

I took her for 3090, computing ethics. Your grade is all projects, in-class assignments, and discussion posts. If you miss more than 3 classes, it starts affecting your grade. Discussion posts are tedious. It can be an easy A if you show up and participate. Gave an extra credit project that gives you 2% on top of your final grade, which is sweet.

The flipped class teaching style doesn't work & the course is paced too fast. We often had an exam, homework, & two pre-lecture quizzes due in the same week which is too much for a 3 credit-hour class like this. She is confusing to follow in practice problems. Worst class I've taken & she made it more difficult and stressful than it needed to be.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--So many papers

review_text: The lectures for this class are extremely fast paced. The professor briefly introduces topics and rushes over every example problem. Tests were extremely rigorous consisting of problems not found in the textbooks/class examples/HW. However, I do think the grading was lenient and as long as you read the text book and do the HW you should pass.

The lectures for this course are close to useless as they are fast-paced and very confusing which are two things that do not go together. When asking clarifying questions during lectures I felt more confused after my question was answered than I did before. Additionally, the test for this class contains material not covered and are very difficult.

This professor put problems on the test that were completely out of the scope of the complexity of what we learned in class. (not that we learned anything in class) She couldn't explain concepts in lecture and every time a student asked a clarifying question she completely misinterpreted the question and uselessly restated her previous statement.

Her class is completely wasting your time because her slides are messy. Also, her style is rushed, you need to do the next chapter's homework when you take this chapter's test this week. So, be nice to yourself, it is better to read the textbook when you plan to go to her class. Trust me, I got 92+ on all three tests and I hardly go to her class.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Beware of pop quizzes

review_text: Do yourself a flavor, and try to take cs2100 with different professor. You dont want this class to ruin your grade, like me. I have friend who took this class last semester with different professor and it was passable. Phillips is the worst professor so far in my uni.

This has been my most disappointing university experience thus far. I think discrete maths is an interesting topic, but I can't help to feel apathetic from Professor Phillip's instruction techniques. You have to learn subjects on your own time, then go to lectures where you "practice" applying these concepts at an unrealistic pace.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lecture heavy--Test heavy

review_text: She doesn't teach everything on the test. The test has questions we weren't taught in class. On the most recent test there was notation that was never explained in class. She had to get on zoom during the test because she realized her mistake, but wasn't present during the actual test.

Honestly the worst professor I've ever had. Class is a complete joke, lectures are essentially useless because her explanations are rushed and do not properly reinforce concepts. All real learning is done outside of class on your own time, which I'm sure makes her job easier, but not yours. Class is heavily weighted towards tests too.
"Prove this massive equation has a solution that is either odd or even. You have 3 minutes"

She's one of those professors who force students to spend unnecessary amounts of time to prove her worth.  The homework isn't hard, just tedious and long.  She also makes us learn the lecture material before we go to lecture with pre-lecture quizzes, so instead of paying tuition, I could take this course on YouTube.  The lectures are also a waste.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Test heavy

review_text: She failed to do -1-1.

I've taken this class from a few different professors. This professor was much nicer and gave some extra credit opportunities than previous professors. The structure of this class was the same for multiple professors so there is no way around the tough speedy pace of this class. I would recommend taking this class with her, she is easy to talkto

Professor Phillips is teaching style is that of a glorified Udemy course, were most of your learning will come from outside resources such as google, and YouTube. Tests had questions on logic not found in the book. Content taught in lectures didn't match the textbook, and the textbook didn't match the quizzes. Grading is strict! Prepare to study!
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Test heavy

review_text: Does not teach course at all. You will have to depend on online resources like TrevTutor. Quizzes (which make up the majority of the grade) will have concepts not taught in lecture be careful of that.
rating_tags: Graded by few things--Test heavy--Tough grader

review_text: Do yourself a favor and don't take her class. The subject is tough enough already. You don't need to torture yourself any farther. Unless... You are really into that kind of thing c: ! But if you do, just prepare to put in the many extra hour by yourself trying to comprehend logic WITHOUT knowing the system. Take Philosophy class before this helps
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: While the professor takes her time to answer questions, often times, her answers were still unclear. Her lectures were often on practice problems. She mentions that we need to do the full step on tests and homeworks but doesn't show us how actually complete the full step. I do not recommend taking her class.

The homework was never related to lectures, lectures were never related to tests, etc. She gave entirely different questions on the test to force students to think of problems differently for every question. It was difficult to grasp onto concepts and apply formulas. Core concepts that should have been covered more were always brushed over.

Prof. Phillips is the worst professor I've had. She doesn't teach. Instead she has you watch Youtube videos posted by TrevTutor before class & does practice problems in class. Your grade is based on 7 in-class quizes & the final with about 10% based off HW. Her quizes are confusing & is nothing like the homework. I learned more from Google than her

Professor Phillips is by far the worst professor I have ever taken. She does not explain things and then when you ask for clarification she moves on without explaining anything. This class is terrible and no one should ever take it from her. I have heard the Math department has a harder but better version, consider that first.
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Test heavy--Tough grader

review_text: Bei is a great teacher and she explains concepts fairly well. The score of CS2100 is heavily towards quizzes which I think is not best weight. To me the course is really trivial, since it claims itself a CS math class, but not math-y as real ones such as math analysis. Be prepared to work and ask if you do not understand, the mileage may vary.
rating_tags: LOTS OF HOMEWORK--Clear grading criteria--Tests are tough

review_text: The grade is heavily weighted towards the tests, and she tricks students because the HW, labs, and study guides do NOT reflect what is on the tests. She does this intentionally to "make students learn how to think."  Also the TA's do not know how to solve problems, so good luck asking them for help. Would not recommend this teacher to anyone.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--LECTURE HEAVY

review_text: Long lectures are hard for me but theres no midterm/final. Its all relativley easy group projects, different from most cs classes. The attendance is strict... only two missed days allowed. Easy A if you go to class and help your group.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Group projects--Beware of pop quizzes

review_text: I have not learned much in this class except that I have to show up for class. Pop quizzes are very easy. Easy A and easy CS Elective if you are okay with showing up to class. My biggest issue is that adding commute this class takes up roughly 4 hours of my day.  Also forcing paper assignments is so stupid, bought colored pencils to use them once.
The random pop quizzes with no makeup is so horrible and a terrible way of teaching.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Group projects--Beware of pop quizzes

review_text: Had a profound impact on how I viewed accessibility. Jason is a nice, supportive person, he is accommodating to students' requests. Class is mostly discussion and presentation (if you're someone who read script, he won't judge you), so attendance is mandatory. And you've to read a lot of papers, at least 4 papers a week, get ready for that.

I am an English BS (COMP1010 is required for my major) and this is by far the hardest class I've ever taken. It's probably not too bad if you're an experienced coder, but it is very difficult as an intro class. I cannot stress this enough: do the extra credit. You will need it and it is not hard. There are 2 coding and 2 written exams + homework.
He is a very poor teacher that does not cover the same information in lecture vs in readings and homework

He's a great lecturer, but I felt like he was taking this entry-level coding class filled with freshmen a little too seriously. He's pretty unforgiving with due dates and just pretty strict overall, which is a bummer because he seems like a pretty stand up dude outside of class. Besides that, just go to class and do your HW and you'll pass easy.

Jason as a person is a great person, and would really recommend talking to him if you are struggling. There is 1 HW assignment every week. He tends to go a bit fast with his lectures, and pushes you to go to help hours with the TA's. If you have a base knowledge of Python and want to freshen up take it, if you're new, be ready to really focus.

Not good at teaching within lectures, MAKE SURE to go to labs. Pushes help hours way too much, majority of help hours TAs are unhelpful and somewhat mean. The textbook and friends in the class was really the only way I learned anything. Even though the class says its for beginners, it kinda isn't.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

review_text: He is a super cool teacher and upfront about the workload. He is clear with what he wants from you during assignments and labs. And gives you a lot of resources to get help. GO TO HELP HOURS and stick to the due date you will be fine.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--EXTRA CREDIT--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Terrible professor he can never answer your question, all the things he can do, is push you to TA to solve your problem. He didn't solve any of my questions.
useless lecture but knowledgeable ta

This class is for beginners, but it is very difficult. However, if you follow every step the professor suggests you do, you will end up with a good grade. Go seek help from classmates and TA when you feel confused. Be aware that Extra Credit will help you a lot! (for real)
rating_tags: Get ready to read--EXTRA CREDIT--Clear grading criteria

review_text: This class gets hard very quickly if you've never done programming -- make sure you stay on top of your homework and assignments from the beginning! + take notes. I'm not the most organized person and I put a lot of stuff off. You'll do fine if you don't, and if you make sure you understand EVERYTHING. Help hours is the only reason why I got an A.

This class is supposed to be for beginners, but is EXTREMELY difficult. TAs are available but don't help much unless you have specific questionsif you get lost in the overall concepts it's almost impossible to catch up & get help. Wiese teaches as if everyone already has a background in CS. If you don't, good luck. Hardest class I've EVER taken.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Tough grader

review_text: Wiese is an AMAZING guy, but this class was beyond difficult. I'm a senior, I've been in college for 5 years, and this 1010 course is the hardest thing I've ever done. Weirdly complex for an "intro". Self-study is important, and going to TA help hours is essentially needed. If you don't HAVE to take this class, don't. But if you do, Wiese is nice.
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Get ready to read--Tough grader

review_text: The course content is extremely difficult, but the organization/layout(two lectures, a lab, and constant TA availability for help) really made this course doable. I think that people get too frustrated by the difficulty of coding and rate Jason poorly but he really is a great professor and honestly extremely patient and generous with grading. 12/10
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Accessible outside class--EXTRA CREDIT

review_text: This course is too hard as a pre-major course! A large part of the knowledge points in the homework did not appear in the professor's lecture!! It doesn't matter if you don't attend class, self-study is even more important in this class!! If you have the habit of visiting TA every week, this course may be more accessible for you!
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Clear grading criteria--Lecture heavy

review_text: If there was even the slightest way I could've avoided this professor, I would've. Turns out the ratings here are true, and he is the worst prof I've EVER had. He doesn't teach anything meaningful for the course, and the assignments are based on your ability to learn to code yourself rather than showing what you SHOULD'VE been taught.

Professor Wiese LOVES giving busy work. Lectures are super long and boring. Doesn't do well with criticism, constantly feels the need to defend why his class exists which makes it seems even less useful. Doesn't grade objectively. And HCI is cool, it's not about the content, he's just a horrible professor who doesn't care about teaching. Avoid.

Jason is a good lecturer, sometimes it's just the slides and the lecture material itself that is super dry. His HCI class is graded subjectively, like a writing class, and that rubs some people the wrong way. But he's a nice guy, and he's available outside of class for help. I recommend his HCI as a class to take alongside hard programming classes.

Short Answer: Don't do it. Long Answer: Lectures are very dry and rely completely on the powerpoints. No in-class discussion whatsoever. He definitely tries hard to be accessible, which I appreciate, but there's never really any reason to go to his office hours considering we never learned anything especially interesting. More business than UI/UX.

Everything  was copied from CSE 440 @ UW. He just changed HW due dates and slide footers. Didn't show any knowledge of material. Only read slides and "umm"s. Very dry. More business and marketing than actual UI UX. Nasty attitude. Made us feel dumb. Unresponsive. Criticize him and you're done. Horrible grading. Rigid. WORST COURSE OF MY LIFE
rating_tags: Tough Grader--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--SO MANY PAPERS

review_text: Professor Wang is an exceptional professor in the subject of computational geometry. His lectures enable the opportunity to build on student ideas to gain a deeper understanding of the goal concepts. Homeworks are difficult, but will help you learn/create variations of several algorithms and prepare you for the exams.

I am in 5150 not 4150. People say Kopta is the goat, welp here's another goat. I took 4150 with aditya bhaskara and it gave me nightmare about algorithm. Somehow i still ended up taking advanced algo because i dont wanna waste last sem on a boring class, and prof Wang completely changed how i think about algo, this class is even easier than 4150.

He has only 6 Homework and 2 exams. The professor has office hours right after class and this makes them more accessible. He also posts a course schedule with textbook sections on Canvas. He also has clear and understandable lectures. Overall very organized and excellent.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: the homework had some interesting problems but graded kind of harshly, exams were easy and lectures were incredibly boring
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Graded by few things

review_text: Professor Wang does not commit the cardinal sin of most professors; apathy. He cares for his students and will do everything in his power to help you. However, he is burdened with some of the worst material I have seen at the U of U. Lectures were tedious and by the second half I stopped going all together. Tests are easy, though (wink wink).

Lectures are long and boring with very little engagement. Content was easy at the beginning ramping up slightly towards the end. Assignments are graded pretty harshly/pedantically. (-1 point for writing k instead of k' even when k = k' kind of stuff). Recorded lectures are nice, and most people skip lecture because of it.
Parts of this class were difficult, but the fact that he records the lectures helped so much. He is funny and helpful.

I found this class unbearably boring... probably the most boring class/lectures I have ever taken so it was hard to pay attention. Just proof examples over and over. Grades are composed of only 6-7 homeworks and two fairly easy exams. I didn't find homeworks that hard, maybe 3-6hrs each. Fine overall, lectures are recorded too.
He is a funny and good professor most of all though the class is relatively simple and the tests are very reasonable.

The class was good overall. While the homework was somewhat challenging, the tests were fairly easy. One downside was that about 30% of the content felt like a review of material from 2420; I would have preferred to cover more new topics.

CS4150 is notoriously a hard course. Yet, under Professor Wang, I spent much less time being stuck and much more time learning. Concepts are clearly divided out and broken down. All lectures are recorded and TA's were knowledgeable. Not necessarily an easy class, but definitely a very reasonable one, especially given the course's reputation.

Professor Wang teaches with a very clear and simple structure. Homework problems come with helpful hints that guide you towards the answer (without giving you the essential part you need to figure out). Office hours were universally helpful. He curves the course at the very end based on overall performance. Overall very fair and well-planned.

Very interesting class. He teaches it entirely on paper in pseudocode which really helps to nail the theory. At first I was terrified of his teaching style, but found he's actually pretty amazing at explaining these difficult topics. The homework was fun, but questions are worth a lot of points so be prepared and don't skip any. Recorded lectures.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lecture heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: Great class that was very interesting and not too difficult. There are only 6 problem sets throughout the entire semester, and 2 exams. The problems sets were sometimes tough, however the exams were much easier. Attendance is not mandatory, and he did record the lectures. Overall the class was very interesting, and I recommend it highly.

I had a very tough time understanding his lectures and would therefore resort to his zoom recordings, which leads me to my next point. He's great at providing resources for his students, he just isn't the best professor I've encountered when it comes to explaining very theoretical concepts regardless of how much effort I put in.
Professor Wang's 3100 is a great class. Lectures make sense, and he makes sure to record them and post all notes and slides on canvas. Exams mirrored the homeworks, which never felt too difficult.

My favorite professor I've had at the U. He truly cares about his students and tries to help them succeed. He always gives time for questions during lectures, making sure we understand the concepts. He has office hours and doesn't talk down to you but speaks to you more as a peer and helps you understand rather than giving the answer.TAKE HIS CLASS
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: Ryan has to be one of my favorite professors of all time. Seems like he is always on Piazza answering questions, and not only are his explanations super helpful but he makes sure that everything is clear to everyone. Also has a super chill personality and definitely seems like a cool person.
rating_tags: Caring--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: Professor Stutsman is absolutely fantastic. His lectures will teach you so much and he is very fair with his homework and exams. He is very responsive on Piazza and is always open to helping you truly understand the material. He made CS 4480 very interesting and I have new appreciation for the topic because of his passion for the field.

Ryan aces every category. Lectures? Fantastic. Organization? Elegant. Grading transparency? Crystal clear. Helpful outside of class? Absolutely. Exam fairness? Exams are hard but fair.  He is so thorough and willing to answer questions in lecture that the wireless lecture which I was excited for got skipped, but that's about the worst I can say.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Amazing lectures --Clear grading criteria

review_text: What I love about Prof. Stutsman is his adaptability. He is amazing at giving great lectures which is most impressive given that this is his first time teaching CS4480. His assignments were fairly challenging, but he is really responsive to students and is super understanding. Loved going into office hours to chat and answer questions. Great Prof!

By far my favorite professor at the University of Utah. I believe this was his first semester teaching the networking class but somehow created some of the best taught lectures (better then some professors who have been teaching their class for years). Clear, concise, informative, accessible outside of class and willing to work through HW problems.
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: Prof. Stutsman is hands down the best professor I've had at the U. He cares deeply about his students, and works tirelessly in and out of the classroom to make sure we understand the content. He is always available for help on assignments and projects, and adjusts the class as needed on the fly. He has made computer networks extremely enjoyable.
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: Stutsman got dealt a bad hand. He was thrown in to teach networks last minute and had little to no time to prepare. Because of that, there are some real problems with the class itself, but Stutsman more than makes up for it. He's an excellent lecturer, and he cares deeply about his students. He's fair with his grading, and generous with deadlines.

Professor Stutsman was a great professor and I thoroughly enjoyed taking OS from him. He is so knowledgeable about the material and seems so genuine and passionate when teaching the course and cares a lot for his students. His assignments were interesting and fun. His exams are hard so be sure to study up for them! Take any class you can from him!

Professor Stutsman's lectures has been one of my most enjoyable experiences in the School of Computing. He never makes you feel like an idiot for asking questions and strongly encourages student participation. He also wants you to succeed and is willing to help. My only critique is that he can go on some deep tangents, but it is out of passion.

I love Stutsman primarily because I have never seen a professor care so much about the content (and the students) he/she teaches. Interesting assignments and fair grading. Should take if you have the opportunity.
Amazing class project
rating_tags: ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: Hardest and most rewarding systems class I ever took at Utah. Tons of good papers to read, great discussions. Hard labs. Attendance is not mandatory but well rewarded discussions. Unorthodox and chaotic, but great ROI. The class is Prof's bread and butter and he really knows his stuff. Explains things very clearly.

Prof. Sutsman's OS class is amazing. He is a hard-core OS person and that shows in the class discussions. He is really adaptible based on the reception in class and digs deeper or explains at a more abstract level depending on the vibe. Hilarious. Accessible outside class. Would recommend for undergrads/grad students alike since it's  a mixed class

Ryan really knows his stuff, and he's very easy to talk to about subject matter. He responds to emails quickly, and you can tell he definitely wants to help you succeed.  Operating Systems is one of the best classes I've taken at the U, not nearly as scary as people make it out to be but still pretty difficult.  You'll have to study hard!

Dr Stutsman is a fun and extremely knowledgeable professor. He has a great intuition for all of the subtleties of the material and always has excellent explanations. Lectures encourage participation from students and Dr Stutsman makes himself available to students needing help.

He cares about his students and wants to see everyone learn something about Networking. I think the class could of been structured better, he taught with slides off of what i'd call his "Networking bible" (Book he helped write). So everything in the class was slide-oriented textbook chapters. He had a VERY generous curve as well. 80% A's.

He manages to turn interesting concepts into boring confusing things we have to memorize for the exams. One of the worst cs classes I've taken at the U. It's a shame because I've heard such good things about Computer Networks while it was being taught by someone other than Kasera.

Kasera means well and can be funny, but he spends too little time on concepts that we get tested on. His test reviews are absolutely worthless, he doesn't do any example HW problems in class, in fact lectures are just a jumbled rehash of what you can read in the book. Expect to be teaching yourself most of the semester. Or better yet, dont take him
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lecture heavy--Tough grader

review_text: I am not sure why he has got such a low rating here! He is an excellent prof! His teaching materials are very well organized. Also, his top-down approach of teaching networking is excellent as most of the juiciest stuffs are there in the app, ip, and transport layers. Keep it coming Prof. Kasera!

He has knowledge of the subject but has very poor teaching skills. During lectures he just writes random computations without explaining anything. When you ask him a question he either says its off topic, or to read it in the book. He doesn't care about students learning, and takes off after class asap to avoid questions. Homework is very vague.
Interesting class exploring many protocols dealing with network security. Be ready to spend some time on homework and programming assignments.
A subject-matter expert. Shows great enthusiasm in teaching the class. Better to attend all lectures as some homework assignments and exam questions are based on class notes.
He has some potential, but he needs some experience. He's still a little nervous giving lectures.
No Comments

I took Fall 2022.   He's a super reachable professor, always on discord to answer questions. His expectations for the class are VERY skewed. He expects students to comprehend things that are not reasonable for the level of the class. I feel like I learned nothing from the lectures because of how much he ranted. He was very generous with the curve.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: Anton is a super nice guy. He tries hard to help students succeed, he's active in Discord, and extends deadlines. But he's super disorganized and has no grasp of what sophomore CS students do and don't know. Get ready for tons of rambling about Rust and x86. I only passed because of Rajeev Balusabramonian's videos, the textbook, and extra credit.

He is a very understanding professor and extended due dates on many occasions. Prioritizes making sure students learned. The tests were reasonable and overall shouldn't be worried taking this class with him.

Anton seems like a great guy, but this class was not organized nor tought well. Most of the class had to refer to outside lectures (youtube rajeev balasubramonian). Tests felt seperated from homework / lecture materials so they pretty little tricky. However, Anton did curve tests and provide a good amount of extra credit and extended due dates.

Anton struggled with setting expectations for this class.   The homework was very easy, but the exams were very difficult and often presented material we hadn't seen before.  His lectures were very hard to follow, and many of my peers and I relied on a previous professor's lectures on YouTube.  I feel very unprepared for CS 4400 after this class.

He was very nice, always extending due dates and giving extra credit. But the downside is that his lectures are difficult to grasp, and his tests are extremely difficult (but graded very leniently). He assumes the student knows a lot more than they actually do. I'm sure he'll be better in the following semesters, as this was his first time.

Dr. Burtsev is a professor that really cares about you succeeding. His first time teaching this course was somewhat disorganized, but he is extremely knowledgeable on the subject and provides students many opportunities to succeed. Exams are somewhat hard, but he curves, provides a large amount of extra credit, and is passionate about the subject.
I loved this class. Everything is clear and well thought out. Fast grading. Professor is very responsive via email. Learned a lot.

Dr. Bagui is the most approachable professor I've ever had in this field. If you are ever confused on anything, do not hesitate to ask for help! There isn't any doubt that she knows the subject (she's also the author of the text, so there's that) and she responds very promptly to questions.
You received the grade you earned.  (No subjective grading!)
Tough course, but I learned a lot.  Dr. B will help you if you're having trouble, so no worries there.  Overall a very good instructor.

This was my 2nd class with Dr. Bagui (the other one was 10+ years ago) and I still liked her, if not even more.  It was an intense 6 week online course, but she clearly stated that from day one.  She responds to emails and will work with you.  Work is graded promptly and fairly.

I had this teacher now 3 different times. Each time I received the same friendly, respectful attitude from her. The work is challenging but if you do all of the assignments than nobody should have a problem making a good grade. I also like that she always makes it very well known at the beginning of the course what she expects from us.
I found the course work challenging, but her explanations were very simple (not simplistic).  She helped to improve my level of confidence in myself.
nothing to say

This class is pretty easy if you have any coding experience. I have heard that it can be difficult without any coding experience, but there are a lot of opportunities to work with either Scott or one of the TA's to learn more. If you don't procrastinate on the labs, this is an easy A

WWU is lucky to have him there because it is not always that a college find gems like he is. He is enthusiastic about teaching and is excited about the subject. He also understands that coding is not easy for everyone and makes sure he is available as much as possible for help. He answers piazza's questions and emails fast. Super recommended.

Scotts a great professor and has a great passion for Computer Science. The only thing I didnt like is he sometimes used class time to push his political beliefs but otherwise he was one of the best professors I ever had.

A really funny teacher who has amazing lectures. Some of the homework assignments were a litttle tricky and might not have been covered in lecture but other than that if you go to the lectures you will def pass. exams were a little hard but if you know the basics youre fine

Took Scott's class for his first time teaching it and I loved it! He explains everything with great detail so the material is clear. While attendance isn't mandatory, there is usually a short quiz (1 or 2 questions) about the material that was covered, but it accounts for very little of your grade. If you want to learn Python, take Scott's class!

His lectures are on their way to good but not there yet. Condescending person for folks new to CSCI. Can't convey information well so note taking is hard. Assignments and some labs were way too vague and for assignments being most of your grade it's not a good time. Too much work for the credit load and you won't actually learn much from the class
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Graded by few things--Tough grader

review_text: If you're in his class you're going to be drowning in code. Unfortunately this is the nature of 241. This guy is an AWESOME lecturer but his class is a lot of work. You will be living in the labs for the duration of his course.

Professor Wehrwein shows genuine enthusiasm for Computer Vision. Both the lectures and the projects were incredibly interesting. The projects were difficult, but Wehrwein was helpful answering students' questions and work was graded fairly. This was one of my favorite CS courses.

Professor Wehrwein although strict, grades fairly. Each assignment has a rubric that he follows as if they were the commandments for his course. Beware of his optional enhancements because they're not so "optional".
His projects are very difficult and sometimes hard to follow. But given enough time they aren't impossible. He has lots of quizzes for easy points and his labs aren't that bad either.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Clear grading criteria--Tough grader

review_text: The biggest difficulty in assignments was figuring out what he wanted us to do based on his vague and confusing instructions. He would often rush through complex topics and expect us to have a deep understanding of them on the tests, which were incredibly difficult. Very poor understanding of student comprehension.

Great guy, humorous and the slides in class are a great help. Honestly did a great job at teaching for his first time and I thoroughly understood the topics because of the way he taught. His class is hard so don't expect an easy A, but if you want to fully understand the content he will do a good job. Mandatory labs, 4 projects, weekly quizzes.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

review_text: Scott Wehrwein was definitely a great professor for this class! His lectures were always interesting, entertaining and had a good sense of humor. The assignments and labs were very difficult at times and took long hours in the computer lab but that's what you have to expect in this field. Overall I highly recommend taking him if you're in CS

For this being Prof Wehrwein's first time teaching, he did a great job. He used powerpoint in lecture which made things a lot easier to visualize and understand. Lab attendance is mandatory but they're fairly easy. The 4 assignments are hard but doable and line up with lecture topics, he holds office hours and he is extremely helpful in them.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria

review_text: It was surreal being in a classroom where you could feel the conglomerate frustration and confusion at the professor's ability to teach. The repeated stress of trying to meet milestones of a group project, to then having it extended the next day in class after complaints that we were not taught the material, was unbearable. Worst experience at WWU.
Lectures were frustrated. Quizzes/exams were random trivia. Grading criteria was absurd. Quarter long project taught me the material but took way too much time and was difficult to do with a partner.

Taking this class with Prof. Sharmin was not an experience I can recommend to anyone. Lectures are extremely frustrating, and it is very difficult to get an answer to any questions you might have. Do not expect to get grades back for at least 3 weeks in this class, and any feedback you might get back is borderline useless. Avoid this course.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Group projects--Graded by few things

review_text: Unclear assignments and grading rubrics. Answers to questions are not helpful and don't answer the question.

This class wasn't my favorite. The lectures never felt really useful and I had to teach myself the concepts before going into the lab. I had a good time working on the assignments, they were challenging but satisfying to complete. The biggest issue I have with this prof is the exams. There's only 2, but they were worth the majority of your grade.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: Lectures were well-organized and informative. Expectations were communicated clearly. Lab work was interesting and challenging. Would recommend and/or enroll again.

Terrible teacher, just bad at her job. Seems spiteful when the class struggles. Your grade does not reflect effort or understanding. Impossible to pass the midterm, she has no interest in seeing any of her students succeed in CS. Can't believe I paid for this. Doesn't pick favorites, she dislikes all her students equally.

Basically everything I don't like about profs at wwu. Terrible lectures, you learn almost nothing, way too much busywork but somehow also not graded by many things, unclear project instructions + grading rubrics, pop quizzes, tests that are just random trivia from the slides instead of actual applicable skills, the works.  Avoid.
rating_tags: Beware of pop quizzes--Graded by few things

review_text: I took Dr Sharmin for 145 and 330. She was a lot better than I thought she would be based on the general rmp reviews. She honestly was very helpful and acessible when you needed her. If you stay on top of what's going on and ask questions you'll do fine. I had issues with my partner for the final project and she as very understanding and helpful.
rating_tags: Group projects--Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: A professor that I can tolerate most of the time. As someone who has already coded I'm java, most of the class felt like a breeze. However, the two exams I did on paper did not feel like they reflected my actual skills. I somehow aced the midterm by sheer dumb luck and the final singlehandedly dropped my grade by a letter.

Lectures are okay, labs and projects were somewhat challenging but weren't too much of a problem. Her exams are terrible. There are only two, the midterm and the final, and they are responsible for most of your grade. They're given on pencil and paper (no, I'm not joking, I wish I was) and are much more challenging than the practice exams.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: If you've never coded in Java, or are new to coding concepts in general, you're likely going to struggle through this class. In class explanations are mostly confusing unless you have some pre existing schema on the given topic. It's possible to succeed in this class as a new coder, but you'll have to attend lecture and study topics independently.

She teaches logic, not code. In-class code is done by her on the whiteboard and you aren't expected to follow along on your computer. Midterm and final are on paper which is ridiculous. The practice final exam had questions about information we didn't go over in class. Midterm practice was not as hard as the actual midterm.

The lecture videos were not very helpful. A lot of the time it felt like we were having to teach ourselves. Theres been poor communication all quarter. We were left in the dark on whether the midterm would be curved at all when the average was 53%. We also found out after the quarter ended that canvas gradebook was actually inaccurate all quarter.

Teaching over zoom is difficult but it also means that being able to communicate with students over email is especially important. Sharmin did not do this well at all. She ignored multiple emails from students and the email she sent after the midterm felt condescending considering how much effort the class put into preparing for the midterm. Avoid
rating_tags: Graded by few things--Lecture heavy--Tough grader

review_text: Dr. Sharmin is a professor to avoid. As much as we can give a pass for inconsistent grading, unclear lectures, and heavy homework load what we can't overlook is her unprofessional attitude towards students. She will blame us, her colleagues, or her curriculum rather than take responsibility. 350 words is not enough to say how awful this class was.

I really wanted to give Sharmin a chance. I had really high hopes for her from what I heard from upperclassmen. I also truly believe that she is not a bad person, however - long story short, over 80% of the class failed her midterm. Made empty promises and ended up half-delivering. Lectures are unhelpful, expectations unclear. Avoid.

I feel like professor Sharmin got a bad rap this quarter. She had a hard time preventing cheating during the final and midterm and the way she conducted the test made a lot of students mad. it might've been annoying but not worth throwing a fit over. I have heard from many people she is great for upper lever cs classes. Maybe not for 145 though.

She is the worst. I did well on every assignment and the final, thinking I received an A in the course, and then she decides to include the midterm where everyone failed (due to lack of time, bad format) last minute in our final grades with MINIMAL curving. The whole class felt like a bad prank. Don't take unless you would like to be screwed over.
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Test heavy--Tough grader

review_text: This class was just one mess after another. The quarter started with bad links, incorrect information and unclear assignments. Has recorded lecture videos then repeats the same thing in class, both of which are unhelpful in learning material. Better off with google. Teacher is uninterested in student well being and is VERY disorganized. Skip her!

This was by far my least favorite class I've taken so far at Western. Her lectures were super boring and not very helpful so I ended up having to teach myself most things. I don't recommend taking this class if you don't have any prior experience with Java and I recommend avoiding this professor if you can.

I second all of the review from Mar 3rd 2021 CSCI 145. In addition, despite the class trying to figure out if a curve/retake/or other solution would be offered for the low midterm which failed us all. The problem was brushed off with passive aggressive replies until finals week leaving the class in distress the entire second half of the quarter.

This prof will ruin your experience for CS if youre not prepared for learning Java on your own. The class average for midterm was 20.8/50 and she passive aggressively said it was OUR fault for not being better at time management. Shes snarky in her emails and the labs/projects are super difficult. Skip CS for a quarter to avoid her if you can.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: From my personal experience with Dr. Sharmin, she takes user feedback seriously. Our class gave her feedback on how we really didn't like true/false questions, so for the final, she didn't have any. She covers a lot of information in such a small amount of time, it makes me wish we had two classes for this since all the info is very important.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Group projects--Clear grading criteria

review_text: 345 with Sharmin was challenging. I think that working remotely definitely added more difficulty than an in-person class would. I ended up spending more time working on Deadwood than actually learning the material, much less putting the lecture materials to use. Didn't consciously put any design patterns taught in lecture to use in Deadwood
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Group projects

review_text: Lectures tend to be all over the place and you're never really sure what she expects you to take away. Asking clarifying questions can just lead to more confusion and obfuscation of the topic. Grading criteria was completely unclear, and felt entirely subjective. Not very responsive to emails. Overall felt like I learned very little from this class

Object oriented with Moushumi was very frustrating. lectures were extremely vague and scattered and when we went over topics she would never follow up with a takeaway or right answer, she would always leave things open ended and students confused. Tested on things that were never once mentioned.

Sharmin's grading is infuriating. The questions were vague and very highly specific answers were required. If you didn't write said specific things, your score was heavily impacted. Very poor instruction, tests didn't resemble lecture material not quizes, projects were lengthy, and was called out for asking questions deemed "easonable by classmates

As someone who has never coded in java before, Sharmin was probably the worst teacher for the course. Her lectures are scattered as well as her presentation style. On top of this, her weekly quizzez are very hard, Especially if you have to take them before lab. Unless you have a lot of experience coding in java, I would avoid this teacher
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Group projects--Tough grader

review_text: Sharmin is a definitely a professor to avoid, especially at lower levels. 145 is an intro course to get into computer science. Yet she doesn't explain topics on the labs till after their due and completely avoids topics you need for labs and tests after saying she will cover them in class. As someone with java history I struggled in this class.

Not sure why she's related so badly on here. Moushumi is exactly what you want a from a CS professor; she explains the concepts clearly in class, is helpful in her office hours, and gives good CS advice. The only complaint I could have from the class is that some of the quizzes were graded subjectively, so I recommend talking to her if it happens.

Grading is super subjective on tests and quizzes an she's not clear what she want's exactly until you get stuff back. Too many open ended questions can make you have to think until you almost run out of time. The project is cool but you will mostly be on your own. Start early because it take a lot of time to finish.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: This was a fun class because of the quarter-long group project aspect. Lectures were a little rough, and sometimes she called students on random. I never used the textbook(s) but I could see how they might be helpful.

There are pop quizzes every week, they are extremely vague and subjective and she's a tough grader making the averages 7/10 and won't curve. Also she will not post keys and isn't fast with grading. The project was fine but the tests will get you because her lectures drag on and tests are very specific.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--Tests are tough

review_text: Summer quarter: 10 weeks of material covered in 5 weeks is doable. Reading is not required, but I definitely recommend keeping up with it as it is so helpful in understanding the lectures. Tests have a question for each major concept, so if you aren't too strong in one subject, you aren't penalized too badly. Overall, work hard and you'll be fine
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: (Summer quarter) 12 week course condensed into 6 weeks with double the people on the project with lagging lecture time when it is needed before starting an assignment.  Too many hands with too little time with too much confusion and not enough explanation.
rating_tags: BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: I liked her, we had 3 projects, 2 were with a partner of your choosing, which worked out really well for most people. I thought her lecture was good, not incredible but i could follow along and i did not have to rely on the book to teach me. The projects were hard but doable, and she gave helpful hints. Also, her tests were really easy.

I think there is a negative representation of Moushumi because her first quarters at Western have been bumpy and inconsistent. In my opinion, she values her students and if you treat her with respect you will learn what you need moving forward and do well in the class and in the future. Admittedly, a poor lecturer. No other complaints.

Lectures are slow and all over the place. Asks students to answer questions and then proceeds without saying whether they were right or wrong, which made the class even more confusing. The final project was creating a GUI in Java for a game that already had a back-end, but she never lectured on GUI creation. Has trouble answering student questions.

The class consists of one long group project which she warned us was a "disaster" the last time it was assigned. The project is mostly busy work and she did not teach relevant object oriented principles soon enough for them to be even implemented as part of the project. Most students didn't finish the project. Don't take her unless you have to.

Communication was tough, I could barely understand her and she never seemed to actually answer/understand our questions. Made easy material way more difficult than it should have been. Constantly contradicted herself in lectures. Would call on people in class to answer super specific questions. She was more helpful one on one in office hours.

Extremely unclear lectures, quizzes and assignments. She tends to ask questions expecting a very specific answer, then proceeds to belabor the class and call on random students when she doesn't immediately receive the answer she's looking for, which tends to waste class time, as does her habit of repeating simple concepts ad nauseam .

Took Data Structures with her and she made the class much harder than it should be. Exams questions are ambiguous. If you justify your answer for  anything on an exam you can go to her office and she will give you full or partial credit back if you have a strong enough enough case. Correct answers may contradict what she taught in class. Beware.

Confusing most of the time. She was very helpful in office hours but doesn't teach very well in lectures and not great at elaborating when someone doesn't understand. Keep up with notes and comparing to the book helped loads.

Prof obviously knows what she does, but teaching is not her best ability. Communication was difficult because English is not her first language and she's trying to explain complicated math and tech terms so it ends up being her repeating the same explanation and getting frustrated that we didn't get it. Dropped my CSCI minor and stopped going.
Fun projects, difficult tests

Tough homework that requires full understanding of the material to complete. Fair tests. Lectures are great. Filip conveys the concepts very effectively and lots of work done on the whiteboard which I appreciate. I learned a a lot and appreciate the care Filip puts into each course!
Amazing professor. He is clearly passionate, and it shows each lecture,

Filip was a great professor. Very engaging and funny in lectures. He would encourage collaboration, in and outside of class, which helped understand tough concepts. I would definitely recommend taking him.

His lectures were good at first but got a lot more confusing as time went on in the class. His perimeters for homework assignments and labs were unreasonable to people new to coding. He limited the number of functions, and lines of code even though it seemed unfair to new students. He also is unhelpful when asking questions/ is very vague.

Was nice and fair with grading. Content was not too hard too understand. Spend a day learning each topic and its honestly easy. Hardest part of this class was remembering the rules for Log and the tricky wording in the tests.

Everyone seems to like Filip; I didn't have a great experience with him. He felt manipulative in how he structured the class--he actually curved down my class by making the cut-off for an A versus A- a 95 after he gave too much extra credit. Similarly he intentionally made things due too early so he could extend them and be the hero. Great
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Beware of pop quizzes--Lecture heavy

review_text: This guy's nickname is J-God for a reason. One of the best teachers around, not just at wwu. Extremely caring/understanding/helpful inside and outside of class, the guy frequently helps students get jobs at major companies and on top of all that his lectures are fantastic and his tests/grades are super fair. Incredible teacher!
Excellent teaching, very accessible and ready to help out students.

Filip is a very skilled lecturer and test-writer. Great choice for a serious CS student or anyone up for a good challenge. His classes are well designed and fair but not easy. Frequent in-class quizzes make good attendance important. As head of the department, he delegates a lot to his TAs, so he can be hard to pin down after class.

Honestly the most accommodating professor I've had so far at western, and he has so much enthusiasm for every single lesson which makes computer science engaging. He takes the intimidation away from learning your first baby steps of code.
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Beware of pop quizzes--Caring

review_text: This prof reminds me why I want to major in CSCI. His lectures were engaging, and he delivered the material with passion. The weekly labs felt like puzzles in the best sense, as we would take his concepts and actually apply them for an in depth understanding. Took this class at the start of the pandemic and he understood that life was rough for us.

I had Filip in an asynch online class, so the format was challenging, but Filip was good about communication and consistent check-ins. The lectures were not super easy to learn from for me, in the end I only read the textbook. He was also really nice in granting extensions. One or two homework assignments per week, usually not too difficult
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Filips lectures were pretty good and are straightforward. You dont even need to know a single thing about programming in order to understand all of his lectures in his CSCI 141 class. The textbook was also useful as well as it was interactive.

Without a doubt the most passionate educator at WWU CS department. He gives each student his full attention during office hours. He is also compassionate as he understands the workload this course can bring. I am lucky to have taken his class whenever I could. Indeed a real gem!

Best prof in the department, hands down. This was my third class I have taken with him, and I have enjoyed every single one. Excellent lectures, clear expectations, and very good at gauging student learning. You won't regret taking any of his classes.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: Filip is by far the best professor I've had at Western so far. Friendly guy, explains things in an intuitive way, and you can tell he's passionate about what he teaches. The exams and homework assignments are all fair, and I felt like I learned a ton from his class. Two thumbs up!

Filip clearly enjoys teaching and that comes through in his lectures, which are very well done. In this class there were daily quizzes/exercises on the previous day's material, so while attendance wasn't *mandatory* per se it was if you wanted to do well. Seconding the coffee/donuts for 8AM final. Great all around.
rating_tags: Respected--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

review_text: Always available for extra help through office hours and email. Really cares about students and comprehension. Bonus- brings muffins and coffee to 8am final

Filip is the best professor of all time, his teaching style is great and he's really caring for the students. Let him help you, he knows what he's doing. He's very knowledgeable of a broad array of computer science concepts, in academia and industry. If you get him as a professor, make sure to take advantage of your money's worth, he's a gem.

The fact that 100 people are in the class does not prevent this professor from teaching everyone as effectively as if you were the only student. His daily quizzes let you know how you are doing and give you lots of practice coding on paper, which you will need throughout CS.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria

review_text: In my opinion one of the best CS prof's I have had so far, really cares and is interested in the topic, lectures are super helpful and will always show what you need for lab and for exams.
rating_tags: Respected--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

review_text: Online textbook is dope, his lectures are good but are more on the faster side. Labs are easy enough with TA, homeworks are manageable with adequate help and time. Midterm and final were very hard. Daily "quizzes" but you can work with partner. Learned a lot and would recommend to others.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: Very good professor. Really cares about students and is super nice. The class isn't too difficult as long as you stay on top of homework assignments and do the handouts to practice. He gives extra credit opportunities and replaces your midterm score if you do better on the final. The tests are a bit hard but very fair

The best professor in the department by far! You will have short quizzes every day but can work with you neighbors.(20% final grade) and lowest 3 get dropped. You have homework assignments that are a mix of book and coding questions(40% final grade and typed in latex). The midterm and final are way harder than the sample ones so study hard.
In class quizzes almost everyday but there is always a chance to talk to neighbors.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: Was my favorite CSCI class I have had. Filip really cares about his students. He always extended due date, you what his extra credit was going to be on the exam and was always there during office hours. He also held extra review sessions with example problems before the mid term and final that where super helpful. If you can GO do GO!
rating_tags: EXTRA CREDIT--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Gives good feedback

review_text: Filip makes custom presentations for every lecture and they're very easy to follow. He's good at what he does and can be pretty funny at times. If I could have him for every CS course I would.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: Fillip is a good professor, and able to explain many concepts during lecture. The weekly homework and lab material does get increasingly more and more difficult however, so be prepared for that. He allows for the use of online resources, and I recommend using google, the python API, and stackoverflow whenever you need help.
rating_tags: Participation matters--BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--Amazing lectures

review_text: This is a wonderful class if you have never touched a finger in programming, like me. Very clear lectures. The material is challenging in a way that is very intuitive and easy to understand why things work. I never read the e-book (which is provided) but I don't think it was vital anyways. Amazing course and teacher for the intro to the program.

Filip does a great job at introducing students to computer science in this class. His lectures are engaging and he is a certified beauty to work with if you go to his office hours. Homework and labs were very interesting My gripes are that we have graded quizzes almost everyday and the tests are HARD. Even compared to the sample exams he gives.
rating_tags: BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--Clear grading criteria--Tests are tough
